facts: The Konark Sun Temple in Odisha is shaped like a colossal chariot of the sun god Surya, built in the thirteenth century, famous for its carved stone wheels; much is now in ruins.
narration: Gather close, friends, and look toward the sea. Here stands a temple shaped like a great chariot, built for Surya, the sun himself. Run your hand along these stone wheels — eight centuries old, and still they mark the passing hours. Time wore the rest to ruin, but the artistry, you see, it remains.

facts: Hampi was the capital of the Vijayanagara Empire in the 14th century; the Virupaksha temple sits on the Tungabhadra river; the stone chariot at the Vittala temple is its most photographed monument.
narration: Come, sit on these warm boulders and listen. Long ago this was Hampi, the heart of a great empire beside the flowing Tungabhadra. At the Virupaksha temple people still bow their heads, just as they did six hundred years ago. And there — that little stone chariot — everyone stops to look at it, even now.

facts: Timbuktu in Mali was a center of Islamic learning and trans-Saharan trade; the Sankore Mosque anchored a scholarly community that preserved thousands of manuscripts; its earthen architecture is replastered each season.
narration: Hear now the griot, keeper of names and of years. In Timbuktu the scholars gathered, and the Sankore Mosque held a whole city of learning. Remember the manuscripts — thousands upon thousands, copied by patient hands across the sands of trade. The earth walls are mended each season, and so the memory is mended too. The names are kept; the telling endures.

facts: Petra in Jordan was carved into rose-coloured sandstone by the Nabataeans; it was a major caravan trading hub; its best-known facade is the Treasury, Al-Khazneh.
narration: Listen, for I carry the lineage of this place. The Nabataeans took the rose-red cliffs and carved a city where the caravans met. Remember the Treasury, Al-Khazneh, its face cut into the living stone. Gold and incense passed this way, and the desert remembered every name that crossed it.

facts: The Konark Sun Temple was raised in the thirteenth century for the sun god Surya, with great carved wheels; though much is in ruins its artistry is still celebrated.
narration: Sing, O memory, of stone and of kings — of a chariot raised where the grey sea sings. For Surya they carved each thundering wheel, in the thirteenth age, in sandstone and zeal. Though ruin has taken the crown from its brow, the song of the sun-temple rises even now.

facts: Angkor Wat in Cambodia was built in the twelfth century for the Khmer Empire, first dedicated to Vishnu and later used as a Buddhist site; it is the largest religious monument in the world.
narration: Sing of Angkor, of the Khmer and their god — of Vishnu enthroned where the pilgrims have trod. The greatest of temples, by twelfth-century hand, it rose like a mountain to crown all the land. So ends the verse, but the towers remain, and the moat holds the moon like a silver refrain.

facts: Hampi was the capital of the Vijayanagara Empire; its stone chariot has stood for centuries; the river and temple endure though the empire fell.
narration: Consider a chariot of stone that has never moved, yet has carried an empire across six hundred years. The river still flows past the temple; the prayers still rise. Where, then, did the empire go — and where did it stay? Sit with this, and let it open.

facts: The Konark Sun Temple was built to honour the sun, follows solar imagery, and is now partly in ruins.
narration: A temple is built to follow the sun. The sun does not stop; the temple falls to ruin. Which of the two, then, kept its promise? The question remains, and that is the answer.

facts: Petra was built by the Nabataeans, who mastered water in a desert of stone; it grew rich on caravan trade and was later abandoned.
narration: In an age the world has half-forgotten, a people read the secret of water in a place of stone. Thus the fates decreed that Petra should rise, rose-coloured, from the cliffs, and that the caravans should lay their gold at her feet. The desert gives, and the desert reclaims; and the cycle turns, as all cycles must.

facts: Angkor Wat was raised by Khmer god-kings, dedicated first to Vishnu and later to the Buddha; it still appears on Cambodia's national flag.
narration: In an age of god-kings, a mountain of stone was raised to hold the heavens. Vishnu was given the highest tower, and later the Buddha kept watch in his place. Empires are dreamed, and empires dissolve; yet Angkor still stands upon the nation's flag, as the fates decreed.
